summary: Muratori describes his 2016 talk at the inaugural Papers We Love conference, where he was invited as the sole game industry representative. He discussed how academic research papers influenced his game technology work, selecting Marching Cubes, Quaternions, and the Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi distance algorithm as his focal papers. He reveals that Marching Cubes played an integral role in the robust player movement code he developed for The Witness, though he hasn't yet given a detailed talk explaining how.
key_points: Muratori was the sole game industry representative at the inaugural Papers We Love conference, highlighting the gap between academic CS and game development communities
He selected three foundational papers — Marching Cubes, Quaternions, and GJK distance computation — as examples of research that directly inspired game technology work
Marching Cubes, typically associated with volumetric rendering, was repurposed for robust player movement code in The Witness, suggesting creative cross-domain application of research
The talk examined how academic research did or did not play a significant role in practical game development
money_quotes: As the sole representative of the game industry at the conference, for the lecture topic they requested that I look back at my programming career and discuss how research either did or did not play a significant role in inspiring interesting game technology work.
Marching Cubes played an integral role in the solution to the robust player movement code I developed for The Witness.
key_insight: Academic research papers, even from seemingly unrelated domains like volumetric rendering, can directly inspire creative solutions to practical game development problems like player movement.
